planting.note: Grown indoors year-round; sow densely on a shallow tray of moist medium.

directions: Broadcast the tiny seeds thickly and evenly across a moist tray of soil or a microgreen mat, then keep covered for the first two days to speed germination. Sprouts appear within a few days and grow best in bright indirect light at room temperature. Mist gently to keep the surface moist without waterlogging, and harvest with scissors once the first true leaves show, usually around a week to ten days. The peppery, mustard-like bite is at its best when cut young and eaten fresh.

planting.note: Grown indoors on trays; soak the corky seed clusters before sowing to improve germination.

directions: Presoak the multigerm seed balls for several hours so they sprout more evenly, then sow them densely across a moist tray. Beet microgreens germinate a little slower than brassicas, so keep them covered and warm until shoots emerge, then move to bright light to develop their deep red stems. Water carefully from below or with a fine mist to avoid crushing the delicate seedlings. Harvest the striking crimson-stemmed greens at the cotyledon or first-true-leaf stage for their mild, earthy sweetness.

planting.note: One of the easiest microgreens; sow densely on a moist tray and blackout for two to three days.

directions: Broadcast broccoli seed thickly across an evenly moist tray and cover for the first couple of days to encourage strong, uniform germination. Once shoots stand up, uncover and give them bright light so the stems stay short and sturdy. Keep the medium consistently moist and provide gentle airflow to prevent damping-off. Harvest the crisp, mild greens at the cotyledon stage in about a week for the sweetest flavor and best texture.

planting.note: Use unhulled seed and soak overnight; grown indoors on trays year-round.

directions: Soak the unhulled buckwheat seed overnight and rinse it before spreading it densely on a moist tray. Cover for a few days while the heart-shaped cotyledons push up, then expose to bright light and mist as needed. Keep an eye out for seed hulls clinging to the leaves and mist them off before harvest. Cut the tender, lightly nutty greens once the cotyledons have fully opened, generally around a week to ten days.

planting.note: Slower microgreen; cracking or presoaking the split seed speeds germination.

directions: Cilantro is one of the slower microgreens, so gently crack or presoak the split coriander seed to encourage faster, more even sprouting. Sow densely on a moist tray and keep it covered and warm for up to a week while germination gets underway. Once shoots appear, move to bright light and water carefully to avoid mold on the extended growing surface. Harvest the aromatic, citrus-scented greens after two to three weeks when the first true leaves emerge.

planting.note: Mucilaginous seed; do not presoak, sow dry on moist medium indoors.

directions: Sow curled cress seed dry and densely on a moist tray, since it forms a gel coat that makes presoaking impractical. It germinates almost overnight, so uncover early and give it bright light to keep growth compact. Mist frequently to keep the fine seedlings hydrated without drowning them. Snip the fast-growing, peppery peppergrass greens within a week to ten days for a bold, spicy garnish.

planting.note: Easy brassica microgreen; sow densely and blackout two to three days.

directions: Spread cabbage seed thickly over a moist tray and cover for the first two or three days to draw the seedlings up evenly. Uncover once the stems have lengthened and give bright light to keep them stocky and green. Maintain steady moisture and light airflow to fend off damping-off. Harvest the crisp, mildly sweet shoots at the cotyledon stage in eight to ten days.

planting.note: Siberian kale; reliable brassica microgreen sown densely on trays.

directions: This Siberian kale germinates quickly, so sow it densely on a moist tray and blackout for just a couple of days. Once the stems stretch, uncover and provide bright light to build sturdy, deep-green shoots. Keep the surface evenly moist and give gentle airflow to discourage mold. Cut the tender, mild greens at the cotyledon or first-true-leaf stage after eight to ten days.

planting.note: Light-sensitive seed germinates best cool; sow shallow on moist medium.

directions: Sow lettuce seed thinly pressed onto the surface of a moist tray, since it germinates best in cooler conditions and with some light. Keep only a light cover for a day or two, then bring the seedlings into bright light to prevent leggy growth. Mist gently and avoid overwatering, as tender lettuce seedlings rot easily. Harvest the mild, sweet, crunchy greens after about ten days to two weeks once the cotyledons are fully expanded.

planting.note: Soak seed overnight; often grown as sprouts or in trays with frequent rinsing.

directions: Soak mung beans overnight so they swell and sprout rapidly, then spread them in an even layer on a tray or in a sprouting jar. They germinate within a day or two and thrive in warmth, so keep them covered and rinse or mist frequently to prevent souring. If growing as tray microgreens, give them light once shoots appear to green them up slightly. Harvest the crunchy, protein-rich shoots after roughly a week, or eat earlier as classic bean sprouts.

planting.note: Fast, spicy brassica microgreen; mucilaginous seed sown dry on moist trays.

directions: Sow mustard seed dry and dense on a moist tray, since it develops a gel coat when wet that makes presoaking messy. It sprouts fast, so keep the blackout period short and move it into bright light within a couple of days. Mist to keep the surface moist and provide airflow to keep the fast growth healthy. Harvest the zesty, peppery greens at the cotyledon stage in about a week for maximum heat and aroma.

planting.note: Soak peas overnight before sowing thickly; shoots can be cut and regrow once.

directions: Soak the pea seed overnight so it germinates quickly and evenly, then spread it thickly across a moist tray. Keep the seeds covered under weight for a couple of days to encourage strong, upright shoots, then bring them into bright light. Water from below to keep the dense mat hydrated without inviting rot. Harvest the sweet, tender pea shoots at three to six inches tall; many growers get a second cutting from the same tray.

planting.note: Among the fastest microgreens; sow densely and blackout only one to two days.

directions: Radish is one of the fastest and most forgiving microgreens, germinating within a day or two of dense sowing on a moist tray. Keep the blackout period short, then give bright light to develop sturdy stems and, in some varieties, a pink or purple blush. Mist regularly and provide airflow to keep the vigorous growth clean. Harvest the crisp, boldly peppery shoots in about a week at the cotyledon stage.

planting.note: Tiny warmth-loving seed; do not presoak, sow on the surface of moist medium.

directions: Scatter the very fine amaranth seed on the surface of a moist tray without burying it, and keep the medium warm to trigger germination. It grows more slowly than brassicas and prefers extra heat, so be patient during the first week. Mist gently to keep the delicate seedlings from drying out while avoiding standing water. Harvest the vivid crimson, mild-sweet microgreens once the cotyledons color up, usually around two weeks, for an elegant gourmet garnish.

planting.note: Small legume seed grown like alfalfa; sow densely on moist trays or as sprouts.

directions: Sow the small clover seed densely and evenly across a moist tray, or grow it in a sprouting jar with regular rinsing. It germinates quickly and grows fine, upright stems, so uncover early and provide bright light. Mist frequently to keep the delicate mat hydrated without souring. Harvest the mild, fresh-tasting green shoots after about a week to ten days at the cotyledon stage.

planting.note: Use black-oil seed; soak overnight and weight the tray to help shed hulls.

directions: Soak black-oil sunflower seed overnight, then spread it thickly across a moist tray and press a weighted cover on top for the first couple of days to help the shoots shed their hulls. Once the seedlings lift the cover, uncover and give bright light to green up the thick, juicy stems. Water from below to keep the dense planting hydrated and pick off any stubborn hulls before harvest. Cut the nutty, crunchy shoots when the cotyledons open fully, usually around a week to ten days.
